output:
# Resolving the MIRAGE Gap in Multi-Channel Retrieval Fusion with TRIAGE: Tri-Axis Calibrated, Context-Gated, Redundancy-Aware Set Selection

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Modern recommender systems for short-video, news, and e-commerce platforms operate under a hard tension between accuracy and latency: complex models rank well but are too slow to score a full corpus, while simple models are fast but limited in capacity [5][7][8]. The industry-standard resolution is the multi-stage cascade ranking system [1]: a funnel of a retriever followed by one or more rankers (pre-ranking, ranking, and optional re-ranking), where each stage takes the top-K from the previous stage and narrows the candidate set from bottom to top. The retrieval stage must filter the entire item pool -- often millions of items -- under tight latency, so it relies on coarse-grained features and avoids heavy feature interaction; the later ranking stages score only hundreds to thousands of survivors and can afford rich feature-interaction operators [3][5][7][8].

Because no single retrieval model captures every notion of relevance, production retrieval is typically multi-channel: several parallel channels each return a candidate list, and their outputs are fused into the single set passed downstream. Common channels include two-tower / collaborative-filtering retrievers [3][4], factorization models [5], item-to-item co-occurrence [6], content/text-embedding retrievers, and popularity priors. The dominant fusion practice is simple and static: either a fixed per-channel weight w_j applied to (often heterogeneous) channel scores followed by a global top-N sort, or a fixed per-channel quota k_j with a union-and-dedup merge. These static-fusion heuristics are ubiquitous, cheap, and -- crucially -- rarely revisited once tuned. The retrieval-stage diversification and calibration literatures exist but live largely at adjacent stages: MMR, determinantal point processes, and submodular selection are applied to re-rank a small candidate set [9][10][11][31], while score-calibration methods operate within a single model [13][14][15][26]. The fusion operator itself -- the point where heterogeneous channels are combined into the funnel's input -- remains the least instrumented, most heuristic component of the retrieval stack.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

Our central finding is that the static per-channel fusion scalar is a conflated object that silently caps system recall. We name the gap MIRAGE (Multi-channel Integration: Relevance/reliability/redundancy AGgregation Error). A single fixed weight w_j (or quota k_j) is forced to carry three distinct, context-varying quantities that have three different mathematical signatures:

1. Calibration -- a per-channel map phi_j: R -> R. Channels emit scores on incomparable scales (cosine similarities, inner-product logits, raw co-occurrence counts, popularity priors), some with no probabilistic semantics at all. A fixed weight bakes in one implicit cross-channel calibration.
2. Context-dependent reliability -- a function g_j(c) of request context. A channel's usefulness varies by request: collaborative filtering is unreliable for cold-start users, content channels are weak for sparse-text items, and popularity helps cold-start but hurts power-user diversity. A static weight is blind to context.
3. Cross-channel redundancy / marginal coverage -- a set-function property. Head items recur across channels; the marginal value of an item depends on what the already-selected set covers. No per-channel scalar can express "this item already arrived through another channel."

The deployed scalar multiplicatively entangles (1) and (2) into one fixed product and is structurally incapable of representing (3). The consequence is a silent recall loss: no error is raised, aggregate metrics look healthy, but recall degrades precisely on tail items and cold-start users -- the segments where channel reliability is most heterogeneous and where long-tail discovery, coverage, and fairness value concentrate [20][21]. The damage is not merely qualitative; it scales as (cross-channel score-scale dispersion) x (context heterogeneity in true reliability) x (inter-channel overlap), and vanishes if and only if channels are perfectly calibrated, context-homogeneous, and disjoint. This yields an auditable diagnostic: measuring the three factors on a deployed stack predicts where and how much recall is being lost.

The corresponding opportunity is a fusion operator that resolves the three axes explicitly while remaining a strict, safe generalization of current practice. We propose TRIAGE (Tri-axis calibrated, context-Gated, rEdundancy-aware fusion): a per-channel calibration map phi_j, a context gate g_j(c) computed from cheap request features, and a redundancy-aware submodular set-selection objective for the top-N. Each axis defaults to the identity that recovers the deployed system (phi_j = identity, g_j = w_j, redundancy strength lambda = 0), under which TRIAGE provably reduces to static-weight linear fusion (and, under a partition-matroid default, to fixed-quota union retrieval). TRIAGE is therefore not a leap away from production practice but a measurable superset of it -- it can ship with a guarantee of no regression and have its three axes enabled incrementally. The remainder of this report formalizes the research idea in the five-question format.

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can a multi-channel retrieval fusion operator that decomposes the single static per-channel weight into a cross-channel calibration map, a context-gated per-channel reliability term, and a redundancy-aware submodular set-selection objective -- while collapsing exactly to the deployed static-weight linear fusion under identity defaults -- recover the tail and cold-start recall that the conflated scalar silently sacrifices?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Fusion is the funnel's recall ceiling. In multi-stage cascade ranking [1][2], retrieval filters the entire item pool under a tight latency budget with coarse features, after which rankers score only a few hundred to a few thousand candidates with rich feature interactions [7][8]. Any item the multi-channel retriever drops at fusion is unrecoverable by any downstream ranker, so fusion quality is a hard upper bound on the whole system's achievable recall and relevance. Because modern retrieval is multi-channel -- parallel channels (two-tower/CF [3][4][5], content/text, item-to-item graph [6], popularity) fused into one downstream set -- the fusion operator sits at the single most leveraged, least-instrumented point in the entire stack.

The loss is silent and concentrates on tail items and cold-start users. MIRAGE raises no error: aggregate recall looks healthy while recall quietly degrades exactly on tail items and cold-start users [20] -- the very segments where CF is unreliable, content is weak for sparse-text items, and popularity helps cold-start but hurts power-user diversity. These are also the segments that carry long-tail discovery, coverage, and fairness value [21][15] and disproportionate marginal business value (new or low-exposure inventory, new users). A single static per-channel scalar, tuned to the head, structurally cannot protect them.

Static fusion weights are a ubiquitous, rarely-revisited industry default. Fixed per-channel weights w_j (or fixed quotas k_j) are set once and almost never re-tuned per request context. That makes MIRAGE a ubiquitous latent regression across short-video, news, and e-commerce retrieval stacks -- not a corner case. Because the damage is invisible to top-line metrics, it persists unnoticed: the field keeps optimizing rankers while the recall ceiling stays silently depressed.

A quantifiable scaling law turns the problem into a diagnostic. The loss is not merely qualitative -- it scales as (cross-channel score-scale dispersion) x (context heterogeneity in true reliability) x (inter-channel overlap), and vanishes if and only if channels are calibrated, context-homogeneous, and disjoint. This yields an auditable diagnostic: measure the three factors on a deployed stack to predict where and how much recall is being lost, and it names the exact intervention target. It converts a vague "fusion could be better" intuition into a measurable quantity with a predicted lever.

Safe drop-in deployment plus a reusable lens shift future practice. Because TRIAGE collapses exactly to the deployed static-weight fusion (index-set equality at default settings; the fixed-quota baseline recovered under a partition-matroid default), it is a drop-in superset that can ship with a guarantee of no regression versus current weights, then have its three axes (calibration phi_j, context gate g_j, redundancy lambda) turned on incrementally. The same calibration/reliability/redundancy decomposition generalizes to any multi-source fusion -- federated or ensemble retrieval, retrieval-augmented generation with multiple retrievers, blended search -- so the contribution shifts future work from heuristic fusion-weight tuning toward principled, axis-resolved fusion.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The deployed object is a single static per-channel scalar. TRIAGE claims to split it into phi_j (calibration), g_j(c) (context reliability), and a set-function F_lambda (redundancy). It is hard because all three target quantities are either unobservable, entangled, or expensive at retrieval scale, and the evaluation signal itself is corrupted by the logging policy. Five concrete obstacles:

1. Identification under logged / selection bias -- the binding obstacle. True per-channel reliability g_j(c) and true cross-channel redundancy are defined over the entire item pool, but logs only record impressions that passed the incumbent funnel. You never observe the counterfactual: would a tail item that channel j alone surfaced have been engaged, had the old fusion not suppressed it? So a naive "just learn the fusion weights/gate end-to-end on click logs" is confounded three ways: (i) no counterfactual signal on items the incumbent never surfaced -- the very tail/cold-start region where MIRAGE bites is systematically missing from the training distribution; (ii) feedback loop and survivorship -- yesterday's weights determined today's impressions, so fitted weights reproduce the incumbent's gaps rather than correcting them (a self-reinforcing equilibrium); (iii) exposure-confounded labels -- popular-channel items get more exposure, so per-channel click-rate encodes the old policy's quota, not channel reliability. Standard supervised fusion maximizes logged-distribution likelihood and is therefore guaranteed to be silent on exactly the segments we care about [23][24][25].

2. Disentanglement without circularity. In the deployed scalar, calibration and reliability appear only as a product: the effective weight is (1 / score-scale_j) x reliability_j, one number. From observed fused rankings these two factors are non-identifiable -- any rescaling of phi_j can be absorbed by g_j and vice versa. Separating them requires an external anchor: a shared calibration target (a common probe set on which all channels' calibrated scores must agree in distribution) that pins phi_j independent of g_j, plus a proof that phi_j is order-preserving so it cannot smuggle reliability into calibration. Without the anchor the decomposition is cosmetic and the per-axis ablation becomes uninterpretable [15][26].

3. Submodular set selection at retrieval scale. Redundancy is a set-function property; expressing it means maximizing a monotone submodular F_lambda over the fused candidate pool under a top-N cardinality (or partition-matroid) constraint. Exact maximization is NP-hard [27]; greedy gives a 1 - 1/e guarantee [12], but vanilla greedy is O(pool x N x sim-eval) -- infeasible when the pool is 10^5 to 10^7 items and latency is single-digit milliseconds. We must approximate (lazy/CELF [28], stochastic greedy [29], channel-blocked or streaming greedy [30]) while keeping enough of the guarantee to matter and without breaking the B1 collapse -- the approximation must reduce exactly to the sort when lambda = 0. "Cheap enough for retrieval" versus "provably near-optimal and exactly-collapsing" is a genuine theory-plus-engineering tension, not a formality.

4. Calibrating non-probabilistic, heterogeneous scores. The channels emit incommensurable quantities: two-tower cosine / inner-product, CF logits, item-item co-occurrence counts, popularity priors. Several have no probabilistic semantics at all (a co-occurrence count is not a likelihood), so "calibrate to a probability" is ill-posed for them. The map must (a) bring all channels to a shared comparable scale without assuming a common parametric form, (b) strictly preserve within-channel order -- an isotonic or temperature map that reorders a channel's own items has destroyed that channel's signal and silently changed retrieval -- and (c) stay stable under the heavy-tailed, sparse supports of count channels. Choosing phi_j per channel-type (isotonic vs temperature vs rank-to-quantile) and proving monotonicity is delicate [14][13].

5. Evaluation is itself biased by the logging policy. The headline metric -- recall on tail/cold-start -- is the one most corrupted by the incumbent's exposure. Naive offline Recall@N against logged positives rewards reproducing the old policy and under-credits any method that surfaces genuinely-new tail items (they carry no logged label). Pure online A/B is insufficient for a first paper: tail/cold-start lift is low-volume, slow to reach significance, risky to ship, and cannot isolate which axis caused the gain. We therefore need (i) a synthetic oracle where reliability, scale-dispersion, and overlap are planted and known so each axis is ground-truthed and the scaling law is measured directly, and (ii) counterfactual replay / IPS with explicit propensities on real logs for an unbiased-ish tail estimate [33][34].

Why it is still tractable. Each axis defaults to the identity that recovers the deployed system (phi = identity, g = w, lambda = 0), so TRIAGE is a strict, testable generalization rather than a leap; the synthetic oracle converts three unobservables into planted, measurable knobs; the context gate is deliberately retrieval-cheap (a handful of flags, no cross-feature attention); and the collapse identity gives a build-gating unit test that keeps the whole construction honest.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The four ingredients (cross-channel calibration, context-adaptive weighting, diversity/redundancy, submodular set selection) are each individually mature. What is missing is the joint operator at the retrieval-fusion stage with an exact-collapse guarantee. The barrier is part structural (redundancy is a set-function the deployed scalar cannot represent) and part organizational (the relevant tools live in sibling literatures that either run at the wrong stage or keep a per-channel scalar). Every near-miss covers at most 2 of the 4 novelty clauses: C1 retrieval-fusion stage over the whole pool; C2 all three axes in one operator; C3 context-gated reliability at retrieval-cheap cost; C4 exact collapse to deployed static-weight fusion.

Near-miss table (each prior art covers at most 2 of the 4 clauses):

| Prior art | Covers | Misses | Why it falls short |
|---|---|---|---|
| MMR / DPP / submodular re-ranking [9][10][11][12][31] | redundancy + set-selection mechanism (part of C2) | C1, C3, C4 | runs at re-ranking on a small candidate set, not entire-pool fusion (no C1); no cross-channel calibration; no context gating (no C3); no collapse to a fusion baseline (no C4) |
| Learned-to-fuse / Mixture-of-Experts retrieval gating [19][22] | context weights (C3) + reliability (part of C2) | C2-redundancy, C2-calibration, C4 | the gate still emits a per-channel scalar, so there is no set/redundancy term; usually no explicit cross-channel calibration; no exact-collapse strict-generalization guarantee (no C4) |
| Score calibration in recsys [13][14][15][26] | single-model calibration (part of C2) | C1, C2-reliability, C2-redundancy, C4 | calibrates one model's scores, not across heterogeneous channels for fusion; no reliability gating; no redundancy; no fusion collapse |
| Bandit / online per-channel quota tuning [18] | context + per-channel allocation (C3) | C2-calibration, C2-redundancy, C4 | allocates a per-channel scalar quota, so it cannot express marginal/redundancy set coverage; no calibration map; the deployed static baseline is not a nested exact special case (C4 only partial) |
| Multi-interest retrieval -- MIND [16], ComiRec [17] | intra-user intent diversity within one channel | C1, C2-calibration, cross-channel C2-redundancy, C4 | diversifies intents inside a single channel's user representation, not heterogeneous-channel fusion (no C1); no cross-channel calibration; no cross-channel redundancy; no collapse |

The barrier, in one paragraph. The deepest barrier is that cross-channel redundancy is a set-function property -- the marginal coverage of an item given the set already selected -- which a static per-channel scalar is structurally incapable of expressing: no value of w_j can encode "this head item already arrived via another channel." This is a representational impossibility, not mere under-tuning. The communities that did build set-aware selection (MMR/DPP/submodular [9][10][11][12]) deployed it at the wrong stage (re-ranking a small candidate set, missing C1) and never tied it to cross-channel calibration or a fusion-baseline collapse; the communities that did build context-adaptive weighting (MoE gating [19][22], contextual bandits [18]) kept a per-channel scalar (missing the set term) and offered no exact-collapse guarantee (missing C4); calibration work [13][14][15] stayed within a single model. The unclaimed cell is a single fusion operator that is simultaneously (i) calibrated across channels, (ii) context-gated on cheap request features at retrieval cost, (iii) redundancy-aware via a submodular set objective, and (iv) exactly collapses to the deployed static-weight fusion -- which is precisely TRIAGE.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Method -- the TRIAGE operator. For channel j with raw score s_j(i) on item i and request context c, define the fused modular value v(i) = sum_j g_j(c) * phi_j(s_j(i)), with defaults phi_j = identity and g_j = w_j. Selection is a set problem: choose S with |S| = N maximizing a monotone submodular objective F_lambda(S) = sum_{i in S} v(i) - lambda * R(S), where R(S) is a coverage-redundancy penalty (facility-location or weighted set-cover form) over item-embedding similarity or channel-overlap indicators; lambda = 0 makes F modular. Solve by greedy (lazy/stochastic at scale). The three identity-defaulted axes are:

- (a) Calibration phi_j -- a per-channel monotone map to a shared scale: isotonic regression for count/graph channels, temperature/Platt scaling for logit channels, rank-to-quantile as a non-parametric fallback; fit on a held-out calibration probe set and constrained order-preserving within channel [14][15].
- (b) Context gate g_j(c) -- a deliberately cheap function (linear or 2-layer MLP) over a small vector of retrieval-cheap context flags: user activity level (new/sparse/power), item-side text-sparsity or cold-start flags, and time-of-day; one reliability scalar per channel, O(K) per request, with no cross-feature attention and no per-item interaction. Default: constant w_j.
- (c) Redundancy R(S) -- a monotone submodular coverage term (facility-location over item embeddings, or weighted coverage over channel-overlap indicators) so the marginal value of an already-covered item diminishes; lambda tunes strength. Greedy preserves the 1 - 1/e guarantee [12]; lazy/stochastic greedy handles latency [28][29].

Two-regime identification (the core of the design). (i) A synthetic oracle simulator with three planted, independently-controllable knobs: cross-channel score-scale dispersion, context-varying per-channel reliability g_j*(c), and inter-channel overlap (shared-item mass). Because ground truth is known, each axis is directly ground-truthed, the predicted scaling law -- static-fusion recall loss proportional to dispersion x context-heterogeneity x overlap -- is measured directly, and the null regime (calibrated + homogeneous + disjoint) is verified to give zero gain. (ii) Real logged data with counterfactual replay: fit phi_j on a calibration probe, fit g_j on context-stratified held-out interactions, and evaluate via replay/IPS with logged propensities; tail/cold-start is the primary signal [33][34].

B-ladder ablation (per-axis attribution). B1 (phi = identity, g = w, lambda = 0), which must equal the baseline, then +calibration (phi_j on), then +context gate (g_j(c) on), then +redundancy (lambda > 0, submodular), arriving at full TRIAGE. A capacity-matched control at every rung (any learned gate must beat a parameter-matched but context-blind gate) ensures gains attribute to context/redundancy structure rather than added capacity.

Datasets (public, reproducible). Build at least 3 intentionally-heterogeneous channels per corpus to create genuine score-scale dispersion and overlap: a two-tower/CF channel (implicit-MF / BPR / DSSM-style [35][36][3]), a content/text channel (item-text embeddings), an item-item co-occurrence graph channel, and a popularity prior. Corpora: MovieLens-25M [37], Amazon Reviews (multi-category) [38], Taobao/Alibaba user-behavior (Tianchi) [39], MIND news [40], and Yelp. The heterogeneity (count vs cosine vs prior) is deliberate -- it guarantees real dispersion and partial overlap.

Metrics. Primary: Recall@N and HitRate@N overall and segmented by tail-item bucket and cold-start-user bucket (the MIRAGE-sensitive region). Secondary: marginal channel coverage / catalog coverage; alpha-NDCG [32] or intra-list redundancy; worst-segment recall gap across user and item segments (a fairness diagnostic); latency / QPS under the greedy approximation (a do-no-harm throughput gate that must stay in budget); counterfactual lift (replay / IPS [33][34]) on logged data; and E_collapse at B1 (index-set disagreement versus baseline, target <= 1e-6).

Responsible-AI folds (advisory, from the team's RAI review). (1) Gate the result on the worst-segment recall gap across both user-side (cold-start / sparse / power) and item-side (head / tail) segments, so an aggregate gain can never mask a subgroup regression. (2) Restrict the context gate g_j(c) to non-sensitive engagement signals and audit that it does not proxy protected attributes; report the distribution of learned per-channel gate weights and selection rates stratified by item-popularity quartile and user cold-start decile, since a reliability gate can otherwise re-encode "tail = unreliable" and deepen popularity bias. (3) Report exposure and catalog-coverage shifts (e.g., Gini of recommendation frequency, intra-list diversity) induced by redundancy-aware selection, and frame diversity as coverage of complementary relevant items rather than content suppression, to address filter-bubble and dual-use concerns. (4) Scope all fairness and coverage benefits as hypotheses pending the segmented empirical validation, not as demonstrated outcomes.

Baselines. Static linear-weight fusion (tuned); fixed-quota union; learned scalar fusion weights; MoE / gated fusion; and post-fusion diversity re-rankers MMR [9] and DPP [11][31]. TRIAGE must (1) match the static and quota baselines exactly at B1 and (2) beat them as axes are enabled; the MMR/DPP comparison demonstrates that redundancy handling at the retrieval-fusion stage (novelty clause C1) differs from post-fusion re-ranking of a small candidate set.

Expected outcomes (honest, modest, falsifiable). B1 reproduces the deployed baseline exactly (E_collapse <= 1e-6), a strict-generalization and sanity proof. On the synthetic oracle, recall gain tracks planted dispersion x heterogeneity x overlap (validating the scaling law), with zero gain in the null regime (calibrated + homogeneous + disjoint) as a built-in falsifier. On real data, we expect a modest but consistent tail/cold-start Recall@N improvement -- an honest low-single-digit to roughly 10% relative on tail/cold-start segments -- with neutral-to-small overall change and within the latency budget. We state the falsifying null up front: if no segmented gain appears once calibration, gate, and redundancy are all enabled, then MIRAGE is not the binding constraint and the hypothesis is refuted.

Falsifiability triad. (1) The collapse test must pass -- B1 index-set equality, E_collapse <= 1e-6, build-gating. (2) The synthetic gain must vanish in the null regime -- calibrated + context-homogeneous + disjoint implies about zero gain; nonzero gain there indicts the implementation or metric. (3) The real-data gain must localize to high-overlap / high-dispersion / high-context-heterogeneity slices, not a uniform lift; a uniform lift implicates a confound (capacity, tuning), not the MIRAGE mechanism.

B1 collapse unit-test specification (CI build-gating). Instantiate K channels and M items with raw scores s_j(i) drawn from heterogeneous distributions (Gaussian logits + exponential counts + Zipf popularity) across many seeds; apply a random missing-channel pattern with the shared convention s_j(i) = 0 when a channel is absent; draw random static weights w_j > 0 and a cardinality N < M. Put TRIAGE in collapse mode: phi_j = identity for all j, g_j(c) = w_j (the gate returns a constant weight and ignores c), lambda = 0 (so F_lambda is modular), and the same deterministic tie-break comparator as the baseline sort (value descending, then item-id ascending). The reference baseline computes score(i) = sum_j w_j * s_j(i) (with s_j = 0 for missing channels) and takes the global top-N by score under the same tie-break. Assertions: (1) index-set equality, set(TRIAGE top-N) == set(baseline top-N); (2) ordered equality of the emitted ranking; (3) E_collapse = |val_TRIAGE - val_baseline| / max(1, |val_baseline|) <= 1e-6; (4) at least 1000 random seeds plus adversarial edges (an all-missing channel, ties at the N / N+1 boundary, a single non-empty channel that must equal that channel's own top-N, and duplicated items). Secondary collapse: with phi_j = identity, g_j = 1, lambda = 0, plus a partition-matroid constraint |S intersect channel_j| <= k_j and within-channel ranking by raw score, the selected set must equal fixed-quota union retrieval (index-set equality). Failure policy: any violation fails the CI build -- the collapse identity is provable-by-construction, so a violation means the greedy or approximation path or tie-break diverged from the sort, and any divergence at lambda > 0 must be explained by the redundancy axis, never by an uncontrolled approximation.

## 4. References and Evidence

[1] Wang, L., Lin, J., Metzler, D. A Cascade Ranking Model for Efficient Ranked Retrieval. SIGIR 2011.
[2] Covington, P., Adams, J., Sargin, E. Deep Neural Networks for YouTube Recommendations. RecSys 2016.

[5] Rendle, S. Factorization Machines. ICDM 2010.

[17] Cen, Y., Zhang, J., Zou, X., Zhou, C., Yang, H., Tang, J. Controllable Multi-Interest Framework for Recommendation (ComiRec). KDD 2020.

[21] Abdollahpouri, H., Burke, R., Mobasher, B. Managing Popularity Bias in Recommender Systems with Personalized Re-ranking. FLAIRS 2019.
[22] Ma, J., Zhao, Z., Yi, X., Chen, J., Hong, L., Chi, E. H. Modeling Task Relationships in Multi-task Learning with Multi-gate Mixture-of-Experts (MMoE). KDD 2018.

[24] Schnabel, T., Swaminathan, A., Singh, A., Chandak, N., Joachims, T. Recommendations as Treatments: Debiasing Learning and Evaluation. ICML 2016.
[25] Bottou, L., Peters, J., Quinonero-Candela, J., Charles, D. X., Chickering, D. M., Portugaly, E., Ray, D., Simard, P., Snelson, E. Counterfactual Reasoning and Learning Systems: The Example of Computational Advertising. JMLR 2013.
[26] Guo, C., Pleiss, G., Sun, Y., Weinberger, K. Q. On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks. ICML 2017.
[27] Feige, U. A Threshold of ln n for Approximating Set Cover. Journal of the ACM, 1998.
[28] Minoux, M. Accelerated Greedy Algorithms for Maximizing Submodular Set Functions. Optimization Techniques (Proc. 8th IFIP Conference), Springer, 1978.
[29] Mirzasoleiman, B., Badanidiyuru, A., Karbasi, A., Vondrak, J., Krause, A. Lazier Than Lazy Greedy. AAAI 2015.
[30] Badanidiyuru, A., Mirzasoleiman, B., Karbasi, A., Krause, A. Streaming Submodular Maximization: Massive Data Summarization on the Fly. KDD 2014.
[31] Wilhelm, M., Ramanathan, A., Bonomo, A., Jain, S., Chi, E. H., Gillenwater, J. Practical Diversified Recommendations on YouTube with Determinantal Point Processes. CIKM 2018.
[32] Clarke, C. L. A., Kolla, M., Cormack, G. V., Vechtomova, O., Ashkan, A., Buttcher, S., MacKinnon, I. Novelty and Diversity in Information Retrieval Evaluation (alpha-NDCG). SIGIR 2008.
